title: Multi-Leg Hedging Strategies
author: Jose C S
keyHighlights: Multi-leg strategies combine calls and puts at different strikes to define risk parameters.
Iron condors and credit spreads allow traders to collect income in sideways consolidation markets.
Hedging costs should be calculated as a percentage of total portfolio yield to maintain efficiency.

content.text: Single option positions are highly sensitive to directional price movement and time decay. Multi-leg strategies allow you to offset these sensitivities by combining multiple option contracts, creating risk-defined positions.

A spread involves buying one option and selling another option of the same type on the same underlying asset. In a Bull Call Spread, you buy an in-the-money call and sell an out-of-the-money call. The sold option reduces the total cost of the trade and hedges against minor time decay, defining both max profit and max loss.

content.caption: Multi-leg options trading terminal showing delta-neutral structures and probability profit curves.

content.text: When the market index consolidates, you can sell volatility using an Iron Condor. This strategy combines a Bear Call Spread and a Bull Put Spread, collecting premium as long as the underlying index price remains within the outer boundaries until expiration.
By structuring multi-leg option profiles, intermediate traders can navigate various market environments—whether bullish, bearish, or sideways—with complete risk control.
